Review

Clinical development of therapeutic mRNA applications
=============================================================

Author links open overlay panel Magdalena M.Żak 1 2 3, Lior Zangi 1 2 3

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ymthe.2025.03.034](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ymthe.2025.03.034 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1525001625002084&orderBeanReset=true)

mRNA therapeutics are emerging as a transformative approach in modern medicine, providing innovative, highly adaptable solutions for a wide range of diseases, from viral infections to cancer. Since the approval of the first mRNA therapeutic—the coronavirus disease 2019 vaccines in 2021—we have identified more than 70 current clinical trials utilizing mRNA for various diseases. We propose classifying mRNA therapeutics into four main categories: vaccines, protein replacement therapies, antibodies, and mRNA-based cell and gene therapies. Each category can be further divided into subcategories. Vaccines include those targeting viral antigens, bacterial or parasitic antigens, general and individualized cancer antigens, and self-antigens. Protein replacement therapies include maintenance therapeutics designed to treat genetic disorders and interventional therapeutics, where delivering therapeutic proteins could improve patient outcomes, such as vascular endothelial growth factor A for ischemic heart disease or proinflammatory cytokines in cancer. Therapeutic antibodies are based on mRNA sequences encoding the heavy and light chains of clinically relevant antibodies, enabling patient cells to produce them directly, bypassing the costly and complex process of manufacturing protein-ready antibodies. Another category of mRNA-based therapeutics encompasses cell and gene therapies, including CRISPR with mRNA-mediated delivery of Cas9 and the _in vivo_ generation of cells expressing CAR through mRNA. We discuss examples of mRNA therapeutics currently in clinical trials within each category, providing a comprehensive overview of the field’s progress and highlighting key advancements as of the end of 2024.

------------------

![Image 5](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1525001625002084-fx1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (135KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1525001625002084-fx1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (135KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1525001625002084-fx1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Introduction
------------

The building blocks of all organisms, from simple viruses and unicellular bacteria to complex, evolutionarily advanced mammals, are proteins. They perform a broad range of essential functions in every living system. For example, capsid proteins protecting viral genomes, hemoglobin transporting oxygen, antibodies recognizing pathogens, ferritin storing iron, signaling proteins like cytokines and hormones enabling communication between cells and tissues, myosin driving muscle contraction, or the coiled-coil domain containing 40 protein (CCDC40) ensuring the function of motile cilia.1,2,3 The information required to synthesize all proteins in an organism is encoded in its DNA, transcribed into mRNA, and transported to the cytoplasm when needed. mRNA then acts as a carrier, providing the instructions for producing any protein the organism needs.4

Protein-based therapeutics are widely used across various disease settings, including vaccines for viral infections, antibodies targeting checkpoint inhibitors in cancer, and insulin replacement for diabetes.5 However, despite significant advancements over time, the production of biologics remains a complex and time-consuming process, making these drugs expensive and limiting their accessibility. Since the discovery of mRNA, a relatively simple molecule, scientists have sought to use it to direct cells to produce desired proteins. If successful, this approach could make protein-based therapeutics more accessible. However, it took exactly 60 years from the initial discovery of mRNA in 1961 to the first fully U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved mRNA-based therapeutic in 2021.6,7 The primary challenge preventing efficient protein production _in vivo_ using mRNA was its immunogenicity, which led to rapid degradation and low yields of translated proteins.

The breakthrough came in 2005 with the discovery by Karikó and Weissman 8,9,10,11 that nucleotide-modified mRNA is non-immunogenic, thanks to their research on naturally occurring modified nucleotides. This pivotal finding laid the groundwork for the development of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) mRNA vaccines and earned them the 2023 Nobel Prize in Medicine. However, other significant obstacles had to be addressed to realize the full potential of mRNA-derived therapeutics. These included enhancing mRNA’s translational potency and intracellular stability, optimizing its production process, and designing efficient _in vivo_ delivery systems. Advances in these areas have been extensively reviewed by pioneers in the field.12,13

Decades of meticulous research into all aspects of mRNA biology by scientists worldwide, particularly the rapid progress over the past 20 years, enabled the development of mRNA COVID-19 vaccines. These vaccines curbed the global pandemic with unprecedented speed and demonstrated that mRNA technology is safe, reliable, and cost efficient (Table 1). This success has set the stage for using mRNA in other therapeutic applications.

Section snippets
----------------

Clinical development of mRNA therapeutics
-----------------------------------------

The spotlight is now on mRNA therapeutics as they are poised to play an increasingly important role in modern medicine. As discussed above, mRNA is a powerful tool that enables patients’ cells to produce any protein deemed therapeutically beneficial. This versatility allows its application in virtually any disease setting where the delivery of therapeutic proteins can provide benefits. In this review, we explore mRNA therapeutics currently under development that have progressed to at least

Discussion
----------

Throughout this manuscript, we have made every effort to collect and discuss all mRNA therapeutic modalities that have reached the stage of clinical trials by the time of submitting this manuscript. The tremendous advancements in this field over the past few years are unprecedented, and we firmly believe that mRNA therapeutics are irreversibly transforming the landscape of medicine.

That being said, a significant amount of work remains, particularly in improving targeting strategies.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: RNA therapeutics target genetic disorders by enabling protein replacement, correcting gene expression, and modifying splicing, offering new treatment avenues for diseases like cystic fibrosis and muscular dystrophy.

RNA-based approaches, such as mRNA vaccines and antisense oligonucleotides, have shown promise in cancer immunotherapy, targeting tumor-specific antigens and enhancing immune responses against malignancies.
mRNA therapeutics are utilized in developing vaccines for viral infections like COVID-19, providing rapid immune responses and adaptability against emerging viral threats.
RNA therapies enable targeting previously undruggable proteins through innovative mechanisms like RNA interference, allowing for the modulation of challenging pathways in various diseases.
Recent breakthroughs in RNA therapeutics, particularly mRNA technology, have accelerated clinical applications, leading to FDA approvals and numerous ongoing clinical trials across various diseases.
